records.ety: [AS. clūt a little cloth, piece of metal; cf. Sw. klut, Icel. klūtr a kerchief, or W. clwt a clout, Gael. clud.]

defs: A cloth; a piece of cloth or leather; a patch; a rag.
quotes: His garments, nought but many ragged clouts, With thorns together pinned and patched was.
A clout upon that head where late the diadem stood.

defs: The center of the butt at which archers shoot; -- probably once a piece of white cloth or a nail head.
quotes: A'must shoot nearer or he'll ne'er hit the clout.

defs: An iron plate on an axletree or other wood to keep it from wearing; a washer.

records.ety: [OE. clutien. clouten, to patch. See Clout, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Clouted; p. pr. & vb. n. Clouting.]

defs: To cover with cloth, leather, or other material; to bandage; patch, or mend, with a clout.
quotes: And old shoes and clouted upon their feet.
Paul, yea, and Peter, too, had more skill in . . . clouting an old tent than to teach lawyers.
If fond Bavius vent his clouted song.

defs: To guard with an iron plate, as an axletree.
To give a blow to; to strike.
quotes: The . . . queen of Spain took off one of her chopines and clouted Olivarez about the noddle with it.

defs: To stud with nails, as a timber, or a boot sole.
notes: ☞ “Clouted brogues” in Shakespeare and “clouted shoon” in Milton have been understood by some to mean shoes armed with nails; by others, patched shoes.
